followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: Minor adjustments only. All three cycle-1 reviews converged on the same core concerns (covariance, Planck-scale phrasing, missing full text), reinforcing rather than revising my initial assessment. I have added Codex's variational-principle-compatibility objection as an independent technical concern and upgraded it to 'major'. I have corrected Gemini's category error (treating a bundle extraction failure as a paper flaw) explicitly. Reviewer confidence raised slightly from 3 to 4 because of cross-reviewer convergence on the same objections. Overall score raised marginally from 4.5 to 4.8 to reflect that the PRD publication baseline was slightly underweighted.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: Gemini's verdict of 'major_flaw' is incorrect: it verdicts the bundle extraction failure rather than the paper itself. A paper published in Phys. Rev. D with a DOI and CC-BY license, received July 2025, accepted September 2025, is by definition peer-reviewed. Calling it 'major_flaw' based on a scraping limitation misattributes the problem.

Gemini's reviewer_confidence of 9/10 is severely miscalibrated. High confidence is only warranted when the reviewer has substantive basis for their assessment; a review grounded entirely in the observation that text is missing cannot support 9/10 reviewer confidence.

Gemini's overall score of 1/10 reflects the review situation (no text available), not the paper quality. The calibration guide specifies 0–2 for 'likely wrong, misleading, or not review-ready'; none of those apply to a PRD-published paper with 91 references based only on a scraping gap.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: Codex: the expected connection need not be Levi-Civita-compatible with any effective metric, so the q-desic equation may not follow from a well-defined variational principle — this is an independent technical concern beyond mere covariance and has been added as a major objection.

Codex: the physical regime (wave-packet localization, backreaction neglect, semiclassical states) needed to justify a single deterministic trajectory is unspecified — incorporated as a missing assumption and moderate objection.
Both Codex and Gemini: unanimous confirmation that the bundle lacks the full paper body, making technical audit impossible — strengthens the human spotcheck list.
Codex and Gemini both flag the 'far above the Planck scale' phrasing as inadequately specified — cross-reviewer convergence raises confidence in this concern.

main_claims.claim: Taking the expectation value of the affine connection operator, rather than the metric, yields a richer and more refined description of test-particle motion in quantum spacetimes.
main_claims.notes: The central conceptual claim. Three independent reviewers agree this is plausible in principle — ⟨Γ⟩ ≠ Γ(⟨g⟩) in general — but none can verify it because the derivation is absent from the bundle. An additional nuance raised by Codex: the expected connection need not be Levi-Civita-compatible with any effective metric, so it is unclear whether the q-desic equation follows from a well-defined variational principle.

main_claims.claim: The q-desic equation is derived consistently via both Lagrangian and Hamiltonian methods.
main_claims.notes: Two independent derivation routes would be a useful consistency check. However, equivalence of Lagrangian and Hamiltonian formulations is non-trivial for constrained relativistic systems with quantum-corrected background data (operator ordering, constraints, parameterization). Cannot verify without the full text.

main_claims.claim: Quantum gravitational corrections to lightlike radial and circular motion exist 'far above the Planck scale'.
main_claims.notes: All three cycle-1 reviewers flag this phrasing as ambiguous. Standard quantum gravity effective-field-theory corrections are suppressed by powers of (E/M_Pl) and are negligible well below the Planck energy. If the phrase means corrections persist at length scales far above the Planck length (i.e., macroscopic), that would be physically significant but requires an unusual suppression mechanism not mentioned in the abstract. The body text (unavailable) presumably resolves this.

main_claims.claim: The framework is applied to backgrounds from canonical quantum gravity and yields concrete, exemplary results.
main_claims.notes: The outline confirms specific application sections exist. The paper is PRD-published, providing some baseline credibility. 'Partially supported' because the structure is consistent with the abstract claim, but no equations or numerics are available to audit.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract; Sections 'QUANTUM GRAVITY OF SPHERICALLY SYMMETRIC…' and 'Q–DESIC MOTION IN SER'

missing_assumptions.assumption: Covariance or coordinate-independence of the q-desic equation must be established, or the coordinate-dependence must be explicitly acknowledged and scoped.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Without covariance, the q-desic equation is a gauge artifact; predictions would depend on the choice of coordinates.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The quantum state of the geometry used to compute ⟨Γ^μ_νρ⟩ must be specified (coherent state, semiclassical wave-packet, Fock eigenstate, etc.).
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Different states give different q-desic equations. The physical content of the framework is state-dependent; without specifying this, the q-desic equation is under-determined.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The regime of validity (semiclassical approximation, energy scale, black hole mass, approximations used) of the canonical quantum gravity background must be stated.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Abstract; Section 'QUANTUM GRAVITY OF SPHERICALLY SYMMETRIC…'
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The 'far above the Planck scale' claim is uninterpretable without knowing the regime. If corrections are only non-negligible near the Planck scale, the claim is vacuous for astrophysical applications.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The claim that ⟨Γ⟩ ≠ Γ(⟨g⟩) — i.e., that Jensen's-inequality-type corrections are non-trivial in this framework — needs explicit demonstration.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If ⟨Γ⟩ = Γ(⟨g⟩) to the order of approximation used, the q-desic equation reduces to the standard geodesic in the effective metric ⟨g_μν⟩ and there is no new information.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Backreaction and quantum uncertainty of the test particle can be neglected relative to the quantum corrections from the background geometry.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: A single deterministic trajectory approximation requires a controlled regime where the test particle's own quantum fluctuations do not dominate the proposed q-desic corrections.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the q-desic equation explicitly shown to be covariant (or gauge-invariant/relational) in the paper body, and if so, by what argument?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Full article text is paywalled and absent from the bundle. Covariance of ⟨Γ⟩-based equations is the central technical issue and cannot be resolved from the abstract.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the paper demonstrate that ⟨Γ^μ_νρ⟩ is non-trivially different from Γ^μ_νρ(⟨g⟩) in the specific canonical quantum gravity background used, and what is the leading correction?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This is the core technical claim motivating the entire framework; requires reading the derivation in the body.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: What exactly does 'far above the Planck scale' mean in context — super-Planckian energies, or macroscopic (super-Planck-length) distances — and what is the actual magnitude of the quantum corrections in the worked examples?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Abstract is ambiguous; numerical results are in Section 'Q–DESIC MOTION IN SER', which is absent from the bundle.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the q-desic equation derived from a well-defined variational principle, and is the expected connection compatible with any effective metric in the framework?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The Lagrangian and Hamiltonian derivation sections are not available; operator ordering, constraint handling, and metric compatibility require reading the body.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Which specific canonical quantum gravity model (LQG, Wheeler-DeWitt with specific factor ordering, reduced phase space quantization, etc.) is used for the spherically symmetric background, and how is the quantum state of the geometry specified?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Not stated in the abstract; requires the body text. The section title 'Q–DESIC MOTION IN SER' may indicate a Schwarzschild exterior region but this is not confirmed.

novelty_assessment: The conceptual angle — replacing ⟨g_μν⟩ with ⟨Γ^μ_νρ⟩ as the fundamental input for the equation of motion — is distinct from the dominant EFT approach (Donoghue et al.) and from purely metric-expectation-value semiclassical gravity. It is related to but arguably distinct from Mathisson-Papapetrou-type spin-curvature coupling (refs 46–52) and from running-coupling RG-improvement approaches (refs 38–43, 67–68). If the covariance issue is resolved, the variational principle is well-defined, and the ⟨Γ⟩ ≠ Γ(⟨g⟩) separation is demonstrated to be non-trivial, this constitutes a genuine if incremental conceptual advance. The novelty is moderate. The authors are an established group in quantum-corrected gravity (refs 15, 21, 52, 67–69, 72, 85–86, 91 are prior work by Koch et al.), so this is a natural extension of their program rather than a surprise contribution. The Hossenfelder video title "Missing Link Between Quantum Mechanics and Gravity" overstates what the abstract presents; this appears to be a formalism paper with specific applications, not a unification result.
score_rationale: Score is held in the lower-routine range primarily because the full article text was not available, forcing all three reviewers to operate from the abstract alone. The abstract raises at least two unresolved technical concerns that would, if unaddressed in the body, materially undermine the framework: (1) covariance of ⟨Γ^μ_νρ⟩ as a physical observable, and (2) whether the resulting equation follows from a well-defined variational principle. The 'far above the Planck scale' phrasing is additionally unclear and potentially self-undermining. Against this: the paper is published in PRD after peer review (received July, accepted September 2025), providing a baseline that these concerns were considered; the conceptual novelty of using ⟨Γ⟩ rather than Γ(⟨g⟩) is real and incremental; and the 91-reference list situates the work in the relevant literature. Reviewer confidence (4/10) reflects severe limitation from abstract-only assessment; the score could shift substantially upward if the body resolves the covariance and Planck-scale ambiguities, or downward if those issues are unaddressed in the body.
summary: Koch, Riahinia, and Rincon (Phys. Rev. D 112, 084056, Oct 2025) introduce "q-desics" — quantum-corrected analogs of classical geodesics — by grounding particle motion in the expectation value of the affine connection operator rather than the expectation value of the metric. The formalism is derived via Lagrangian and Hamiltonian methods and applied to spherically symmetric static backgrounds from canonical quantum gravity, with worked examples for lightlike radial and circular motion. This cycle 2 review incorporates input from two other reviewers (Codex, Gemini). All three cycle-1 reviews converge on the same assessment: the bundle contains only the abstract and references, so no derivation, equation, or numerical result is auditable. The paper is published and peer-reviewed in PRD; the low scores reflect review limitations, not a finding that the paper is wrong.

technical_objections.evidence: The classical geodesic equation is covariant because the inhomogeneous transformation piece of Γ cancels with the derivative of the tangent vector. Taking a quantum expectation value does not automatically restore covariance; the abstract makes no mention of how this is handled.
technical_objections.objection: The affine connection is not a generally covariant tensor; ⟨Γ^μ_νρ⟩ is coordinate-dependent. The q-desic equation is physically meaningful only if the paper demonstrates covariance (or explicitly restricts to a gauge-fixed or relational setting).

technical_objections.evidence: For a generic quantum state, ⟨Γ⟩ need not be metric-compatible, may have torsion or nonmetricity, and may not be generated by any point-particle action. Codex independently raised this concern. If the q-desic equation does not follow from a Lagrangian in the usual sense, the Lagrangian/Hamiltonian derivation claim requires careful qualification.
technical_objections.objection: It is unclear whether the expectation-value connection is Levi-Civita-compatible with any effective metric, or whether the q-desic equation follows from a well-defined variational principle.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract: 'derive the q–desic equation using both Lagrangian and Hamiltonian methods'

technical_objections.evidence: All three cycle-1 reviewers independently flag this. If super-Planckian energies are meant, the effective-field-theory framework used to derive the corrections breaks down there. If sub-Planckian energy but super-Planck-length (macroscopic) corrections are meant, the phrasing is misleading and a non-standard enhancement mechanism is required.
technical_objections.objection: The claim of corrections 'far above the Planck scale' is physically unclear or potentially self-undermining. Standard EFT quantum gravity corrections are suppressed by (E/M_Pl)^n and negligible below the Planck scale.

technical_objections.evidence: A single deterministic trajectory requires specification of semiclassical states, wave-packet localization, neglect of backreaction, and a relational time/coordinate. These conditions are unstated. Codex independently raised this.
technical_objections.objection: The physical regime in which a single test-particle q-desic trajectory applies — rather than a superposition, decohered branch, or distribution — is not specified in the abstract.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract: 'motion of test particles in quantum-gravitational backgrounds'

technical_objections.evidence: The reference list cites Donoghue (refs 26, 78), Bjerrum-Bohr et al. (ref 80), Faller (ref 77), and Hu–Verdaguer stochastic gravity (refs 34–35). These approaches already compute quantum corrections to classical trajectories. Without the introduction, the differentiating argument is not auditable.
technical_objections.objection: The paper's conceptual novelty over prior semiclassical gravity (Hu–Verdaguer stochastic gravity), EFT quantum corrections (Donoghue), and RG-improvement approaches (Reuter–Saueressig, Bonanno–Reuter) is not established in the abstract.
